primary_diagnosis.name: Acute non-specific mechanical lower back pain with paraspinal muscle spasm

pathophysiology_summary: A sudden mechanical trigger (forward flexion) provoked a protective reflex spasm of the paraspinal (erector spinae) muscles. Nociceptive input from strained musculoligamentous structures drives sustained involuntary muscle contraction, which itself becomes painful (ischaemic metabolite accumulation), producing a self-reinforcing pain-spasm cycle and the board-like rigidity and near-total immobility described. There is no structural neural compromise: the intact bladder/bowel function, absent saddle anaesthesia, absent radicular symptoms and normal lower-limb neurology place this as benign, self-limiting, non-specific low back pain rather than cauda equina syndrome, a compressive radiculopathy, or a fracture. The natural history is favourable — spasm peaks over 24–48 hours then eases, with functional recovery over days and residual ache resolving over 2–4 weeks. Early gentle mobilisation shortens recovery; strict bed rest prolongs it.

differential.diagnosis: acute non-specific mechanical low back pain with muscle spasm
differential.evidence_basis: Sudden onset after trivial flexion, band-like bilateral lumbosacral pain, rock-hard paraspinal tightening, relief only with recumbency — the classic mechanical spasm pattern.
Any acute severe low back pain with profound immobility mandates an active cauda equina screen; must be excluded by history before it can be down-weighted.
A disc herniation can present after flexion, but would be expected to radiate below the knee in a dermatomal pattern.
Possible with osteoporosis or trauma, but the mechanism was trivial flexion with no fall and no midline bony tenderness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI Doctor should hold the benign mechanical diagnosis as leading while actively running the cauda equina and sinister-feature screens before committing to home management.

differential.diagnosis: acute non-specific mechanical low back pain with muscle spasm
differential.evidence_basis: Negative cauda equina screen (DI-003), no radicular features (DI-002), no sinister features (DI-004) — benign mechanical spasm confirmed on history.
Continence intact, no saddle anaesthesia, no progressive leg weakness — no history features of cauda equina.
No pain radiating below the knee, no dermatomal sensory or motor deficit reported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once the red-flag screen is negative the correct move is de-escalation to reassurance and self-care, NOT further work-up or imaging.

differential.diagnosis: acute non-specific mechanical low back pain with paraspinal muscle spasm
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, self-limiting mechanical spasm with a negative red-flag screen; the only outstanding issue is functional (immobility while living alone), not diagnostic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a confident benign diagnosis with conservative management and clear cauda equina safety-netting; the immobility is a practical-support problem, not a clinical escalation.

red_flags.description: Cauda equina features — urinary retention or incontinence, faecal incontinence, saddle/perineal anaesthesia, bilateral or progressive leg weakness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cauda equina syndrome is a neurosurgical emergency; decompression within hours preserves bladder, bowel and sexual function. Screened negative here by history.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these features later develop and are not escalated immediately, delayed decompression causes permanent incontinence, saddle numbness and leg weakness.

red_flags.description: Sinister-cause features — significant trauma, history of malignancy, fever/night sweats/weight loss, unrelenting night pain, age extremes
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would raise suspicion of fracture, spinal infection (discitis/epidural abscess) or malignancy, changing management from reassurance to urgent imaging/referral.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed spinal infection or metastatic lesion can progress to cord compression or sepsis.

red_flags.description: New severe radicular pain below the knee or new focal leg weakness (compressive radiculopathy)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate nerve-root compression needing assessment and possibly imaging if progressive or with a neurological deficit.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive motor deficit (e.g. foot drop) can become permanent if not assessed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swayed by the vivid immobility and 'stuck on the floor' framing into a 000/ED escalation, when the red-flag screen is negative and the condition is benign and self-limiting.

Ordering lumbar X-ray/CT/MRI for acute non-specific low back pain without red flags — imaging is not indicated, does not change management, and exposes the patient to radiation and incidental-finding cascades.
Advising strict bed rest, which weakens supporting muscles, prolongs spasm and delays recovery; early gentle mobilisation is correct.
Escalating straight to opioids for a benign mechanical spasm — simple analgesia (paracetamol +/- an NSAID) is first-line; opioids add sedation, constipation and dependence risk with little benefit.
Under-reacting to the benign label and failing to give explicit bladder/bowel/saddle-numbness 000 safety-netting — the one time-critical miss in an otherwise benign case.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lower-limb power, tone and deep tendon reflexes (patellar, Achilles)
Straight leg raise / neural tension testing
Perianal sensation and anal sphincter tone
Palpation for midline bony point tenderness or a step-off deformity
Objective measured vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate) without a home device
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: No emergency escalation needed. Confirm the benign diagnosis via a history-based red-flag screen, give conservative self-care and analgesia advice, arrange practical assistance to get off the floor safely (a family member/neighbour, or a non-emergency lift-assist if truly alone and unable), and provide explicit cauda equina safety-netting with a same-day pathway for new radicular or sinister features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the benign diagnosis when the history screen is negative. The genuine residual risk is an evolving cauda equina or compressive radiculopathy that is not yet manifest — which is exactly why the history screen must be documented and the safety-net given, even though the working diagnosis is benign.
